notes: v1 — synthesized from 12 extracted prompt-pattern candidates + a manual pass over all 540 ELF questions (27 exams). Eight cognitive-operation types. Per persona-pass findings, ELF is more uniform than LÄS (mean 4.67 vs 4.11) and the prompt language is highly stereotyped — most questions classify cleanly into ONE type via the prompt verb alone (told/said vs implied vs concluded vs main vs tone/attitude vs why/purpose vs in-line-with vs expression). Highest-leverage trajectory finding: stance/tone and fine-distinction inference under dense academic prose are the persistent v3 error pockets — protocols for ELF-TYPE-002 and ELF-TYPE-005 reflect that. ELF-TYPE-008 (vocabulary-in-context) has exactly 5 corpus examples and sits at the floor; flagged for v2 expansion if more surface.

attack_protocol: Lock the prompt's target noun phrase (e.g. 'Margaret Cavendish', 'the medical effects of music'). It tells you where in the passage to look.
Scan the passage for that noun phrase or its closest synonym; underline the surrounding sentence.
Read ONLY that sentence + the one before and after. Do not pull from elsewhere.
Match each option to that local span word-by-word. The correct option paraphrases ONE sentence with no additions.
Reject any option that adds quantity, cause, or scope the text doesn't supply, even if it sounds reasonable.

common_distractors.pattern: True statement from the wrong paragraph
common_distractors.why_it_traps: The fact is real but stated about a different entity than the prompt names; students who skim recognize the words and pick it.

common_distractors.pattern: Real-world common knowledge not in the text
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Sounds obviously true (e.g. 'music can be relaxing') so the student picks it; but the text never says so and ELF is text-grounded only.
Option keeps the text's words but strengthens a hedged claim to an absolute; the surface match feels right.

common_distractors.pattern: Causal claim grafted onto descriptive statement
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Text describes X happening; option adds 'because Y' that the text only mentioned in passing nearby.

entries.notes: Recognize by prompt verbs: 'we are told', 'is said', 'is stated', 'according to the text' WITHOUT 'main' or 'implied'. ~101 corpus questions — the largest single bucket. Lowest-difficulty type when read carefully; high-difficulty only when the passage contains multiple similar-sounding spans.

attack_protocol: Read the prompt: 'implied' = the answer is NOT literally stated; you must combine sentences or read between the lines.
Locate the local span anchored by the prompt's target (paragraph, named entity, or 'here').
Identify the writer's UNSAID-BUT-NECESSARY assumption — the bridge between two statements, or what is needed for the explicit claim to make sense.
Find the option that follows from the span as a minimal additional step. The correct option is almost always one logical inch beyond the text, never two.
Drop any option that is either literally restated in the text (too close — that's retrieval, not inference) OR requires outside knowledge / a leap of two steps.

common_distractors.why_it_traps: Looks safe because it matches the text word-for-word; students avoid the inferential leap and pick the surface match — but 'implied' demands it NOT be literal.
The option follows IF you also assume something the text never gave you; sounds intelligent and worldly.
Text implies A causes B; option says B causes A or A prevents B; sign flips under pressure.
Text implies a thing about ONE case; option generalizes to all cases of that kind.

entries.notes: Recognize by 'implied' (~56 corpus questions). Persona-pass flagged this as the high-error type when prose is dense and academic. Calibration rule: if the option appears verbatim in the passage, it is NOT the implication answer.

attack_protocol: 'Concluded' = synthesize across the named span (a paragraph, two paragraphs, or the whole text) — NOT just one sentence.
Skim the named span and note 2-4 supporting points. Look for the connective tissue (however, therefore, thus, in other words).
Predict the conclusion in your own words BEFORE looking at options.
Pick the option that fits ALL the supporting points, not just one. A conclusion that only matches the last sentence is a trap.
Reject options that contradict any single sentence in the span, even if they paraphrase the gist.

common_distractors.pattern: Conclusion drawn from only the final sentence
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Students anchor on the last thing they read; the option restates the closing line but ignores qualifiers earlier in the span.

common_distractors.pattern: Conclusion that overstates a hedged premise
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Span uses 'may', 'tends to', 'in some cases'; option upgrades to a flat assertion that doesn't survive the qualifiers.
Span discusses two phenomena; option covers one and ignores the other, so it's defensible-looking but incomplete.

common_distractors.pattern: Conclusion borrowed from a different paragraph
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Prompt names 'the first paragraph' or 'the last two paragraphs' specifically; the option states something true elsewhere in the text.

entries.notes: Recognize by 'can be concluded' / 'may be concluded' (~32 corpus questions). Distinct from ELF-TYPE-002 (inference) in that conclusion synthesizes EXPLICIT statements; inference fills in unstated bridges. Distinct from ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea) in that conclusion targets a NAMED SPAN, not the whole text's thesis.

attack_protocol: Decide the scope: 'main point of the text' = whole passage; 'main point of the opening paragraph' = just that paragraph.
For whole-text questions: locate the thesis sentence (often paragraph 1's last sentence OR the final paragraph's first sentence) and the recurrent subject across paragraphs.
For paragraph-scope questions: find the sentence the other sentences in that paragraph support.
Pick the option that captures the WIDEST claim still supported by the span; reject options that are merely true about one sub-detail.
Reject options too narrow (a single supporting fact) AND too broad (a slogan beyond what the text argues).

common_distractors.pattern: Supporting detail dressed as main point
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Option restates a vivid example from the text; students remember the example and pick it instead of the abstraction it illustrates.

common_distractors.pattern: Slogan that overshoots the text's scope
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Sounds like a thesis but generalizes beyond what the writer actually argues; the writer covered narrower ground.
Option names what the text is ABOUT (the topic) rather than what it CLAIMS (the thesis); both feel right when you only registered the subject.

common_distractors.pattern: Counter-position that the writer mentions but rejects
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Text presents a view to argue against it; student remembers the view's content and picks it as 'main point'.

entries.notes: Recognize by 'main point / main argument / main claim / main focus / main conclusion / main impression' (~43 corpus questions, plus a few 'main purpose'). High-leverage type — perfect-score students nail these by anchoring on the thesis sentence rather than memorable details.

attack_protocol: Identify whose stance: writer's overall, a named figure's, or the text's tone as a whole? The prompt names the target.
Scan for evaluative language: adjectives, adverbs, hedges, irony markers, scare quotes, exclamations, rhetorical questions.
Map the evaluative load on a 2-axis grid: positive/negative AND committed/hedged. The correct option lands on the same coordinates.
For a named figure: find direct quotes / paraphrases attributed to them; the writer's stance and the figure's stance are NOT the same — separate them.
Reject options that pick up the SUBJECT's tone instead of the WRITER's tone (e.g. a text about anger isn't necessarily angry).

common_distractors.why_it_traps: Text describes a sentimental film or a sarcastic speaker; option labels the TEXT sentimental or sarcastic when the writer is actually detached.
Writer is 'mildly critical' or 'cautiously positive'; option says 'scathing' or 'enthusiastic' — same sign, wrong intensity.
Writer is 'impressed but reserved' or 'positive with serious objections'; option captures only one half and reads as plausible.
Writer quotes a critic; option uses the critic's evaluative words and assigns them to the writer, who actually disagrees.

entries.notes: Recognize by 'tone', 'attitude', 'characterized', 'best be described', 'reviewer’s' / 'writer’s overall' (~23 corpus questions). Trajectory tagged this as a high-error type even in v3 — fine distinctions between e.g. 'on the whole positive but has some serious objections' vs 'clearly impressed but avoids taking a stand' are the persistent failure mode.

attack_protocol: Decide the question kind: 'why is X mentioned' (rhetorical purpose) OR 'what caused Y' (in-text causal chain).
For rhetorical purpose: find the sentence WHERE the element appears, then ask what argumentative work it does in the paragraph (illustration, contrast, evidence, hook).
For causal: locate the chain of events; identify the trigger the text names (not the most plausible real-world cause).
Match the option to the textual chain — not your prior beliefs about why things happen.
Reject options that describe a true consequence rather than the cause, or vice versa (direction flips).

common_distractors.pattern: Plausible real-world cause not in the text
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Option states a cause that fits world knowledge; ELF requires the cause the WRITER attributes, which may differ.
Option swaps direction in the causal chain; both items are in the text so option feels familiar.
Asked why X is mentioned, option says 'because the text is about X' — true but vacuous; the actual purpose is rhetorical (illustration, contrast, etc.).

common_distractors.pattern: Local cause when the text frames a deeper one
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Option names the proximate trigger; text emphasizes a background condition as the 'basic' cause (and the prompt often uses 'basically' / 'primarily' to flag this).

entries.notes: Recognize by 'why', 'main reason', 'purpose', 'caused', 'why is X mentioned' (~19 corpus questions combined). Two sub-flavors share an attack protocol because both ask 'identify the writer's explicit attribution'. Smallish bucket but distinctive enough to keep separate — purpose/cause are not interchangeable with retrieval (no surface phrase to match) or inference (the text names the cause).

attack_protocol: Recognize the open-ended frame: the prompt does not name a target span ('in line with the text', 'in agreement with the text', 'true according to the text').
Read all options first to extract candidate claims; scan the text for each claim's keyword anchors.
For each option, locate its anchor span and check whether the text actually supports the claim — supports, contradicts, or doesn't mention.
Eliminate any option the text contradicts OR doesn't mention. The correct option is the one the text supports verbatim or by near-paraphrase.
When two options seem supported, prefer the one matching the text's qualifiers (hedges, scopes, exceptions); the other usually overreaches.

common_distractors.pattern: True in the real world but not in the text
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Common knowledge that the text never asserts; students conflate world knowledge with text content.
Text says 'in some grammar schools'; option says 'in all'; student matches the topic and misses the quantifier.

common_distractors.pattern: Plausible extension the text doesn't make
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Sounds like the natural next claim a writer would make; but it crosses from 'supports' to 'compatible with' — alignment demands explicit support.
Option flips a key qualifier (no → some, decrease → increase, before → after); reads correctly under speed-reading.

entries.notes: Recognize by 'which of the following statements is most in line with' / 'in agreement with' / 'is true according to' (~24 corpus questions). Distinct from ELF-TYPE-001 retrieval in that the prompt does NOT name what to look up — you must let the options drive your scan. Time-expensive type; allocate up to 4 lookups per question.

attack_protocol: Lock the quoted expression in the prompt (e.g. 'time is money', 'white elephant', 'survival benefits').
Locate the expression in the passage; read the 2-3 sentences around it for context — the text always defines/uses the expression in-context.
Predict the meaning IN THIS CONTEXT before looking at options; do not import the standard idiomatic meaning if the text is using it differently.
Match the option whose paraphrase fits the contextual usage, not the most common dictionary sense.
Reject options that paraphrase the literal-words meaning (a 'white elephant' is not literally about an elephant) or the most-common idiomatic sense if the text bends it.

common_distractors.pattern: Literal reading of the figurative expression
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Option treats 'white elephant' / 'survival benefits' / 'time is money' word-for-word; appealing when context is dense and student grabs surface meaning.

common_distractors.pattern: Standard idiom meaning vs the text's bent usage
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Option gives the dictionary sense; text uses the expression in a culturally-specific or technical sense that diverges.
Option swaps the expression for a related-but-not-equivalent concept (e.g. 'time is money' → 'time is valuable' when text means 'time should be spent productively').

entries.notes: Recognize by quoted expression in the prompt + verbs 'best corresponds to', 'is said about the expression', 'is implied about the expression', 'reflects'. Only 5 corpus instances — at the floor for a type. Flagged for v2: expand only if the corpus grows; otherwise consider folding into ELF-TYPE-001 or ELF-TYPE-002 by context.
